channel.title: Comments for page "The Clock Shall Strike Again"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "The Clock Shall Strike Again"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh damn. Shouldn't have directed your attention to that present tense…! ;-D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well the change of tenses is still there, just that I don't really intend to change them within the same paragraph/small parts. I think if I change in like, every sentence, it will be a bit too confusing. Honestly I didn't even change that much stuff from your first read? Or like that few places are like, all the best parts? But I mean, totally understand if you think this is not enough to carry the sense of disjointed time.

(I think the And so the crows laughed actually has a big chuck of story? Or do you actually mean Midnight Parade…)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think both this and Midnight Parade suffer from being a merely fragmentary narrative that doesn't really progress beyond exposition. This is maybe the start of a story, but it lacks conflict, climax or resolution. It is more of an atmospheric prose poem, than a short story. Evaluated as a prose poem it isn't bad, and the illustrations are, of course, absolutely excellent. I think that I would rather this were developed more as a narrative, though.

I am also a bit disappointed that the thing that I found most interesting on the first read, the sense of disjointed time, was the result of error.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It does look interesting, but I still don't want to messes up the tense too much. I'll just keep the tale like what it is now. What I mean is like, it's a great idea, and you can use it in your own writing if you're up to it :P

And since you pointed that out, changed the second part of the first sentence into future tense as well, it's not done intentionally.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do admit it is a, um, bold idea, that would be difficult to pull through! ;>

Mmh. Lemme try something… Let's see… With the last paragraph, for instance…

The clock shall strike zero, and the guard and the gate are no longer there. The King was hanging dead and the Dragon emerges again. The clock shall point to zero, and all ended.

My changes in italics. You did mix up future and present in the first sentence!!
You still have some logic (the clock shall strike, the clock shall point) and no bold innovation (though I am tempted to write 'all shall ended' ;P)… but the tenses are mixed up.
I think it's OK, but I will understand if you don't! ;>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really glad that you like it! (But yeah I should have done a better job at proofreading :P)

As for mixing up the tenses more and more, yeah, it's a good idea, but it's aslo extremely difficult, and I don't think I can pull that off myself. I think there's a balance between making sense and being disorientating, and what I have now is already a bit confusing for at least part of the readers. I aslo do want to keep at least some order there as I require a repetitive structure for the whole countdown thing. But it is nevertheless a good idea and I think it can be employed in other places :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now that, my friend, is writing.
It is extremely difficult to successfully give priority to writing over story/characters, but I think you've done it well here.
I disagree with Kalinin - except re: the typos, which have been corrected in the meantime. Of course the tense changes are confusing; this is, IMO, a deliberate effect. The scene is confusing - or perhaps I should say the events are confusing; the narrator is confused; so the reader should be confused. Nothing wrong with that if it's well done, and I do think it is.
I don't know the context yet, and I enjoyed the tale anyway. Gonna have myself some reading, and come back to this afterwards; shall keep you posted on whether it consequently deserves +5, or if I'll stick to my single upvote! <grin>

Oh, just a thought: me, I would have mixed up tenses, more and more as we come closer to the end of reality, past/present/future etc. (even combinations like, for instance, will guarded) all jumbled including within sentences, to render the chaos even more. What do you think?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for pointing this out, fixed what I can find. The change in tense within the same paragraph isn't what I intended to do, sorry about those, hopefully it's better now and less confusing. The change of tense should occur when move on to the next section.

It's true that there isn't that much of the story in it, what I was trying to do is to use the tenses to represent the chaotic passage of time + ties things togther. Hopefully by fixing the grammar the former intention can be carried out. I mean, I won't say there's no plot at all, but yeah, totally valid to say there is not much of a story.

Thanks a lot for the critique :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ This could have used a proofreading.
- hide text

The clock stroke twelve

The clock struck twelve.

their faces covered by masks of masquerade

"Masks of masquerade" isn't a mistake, per se, but it is way too repetitive.

The guard will be standing there as the mob drag the twisted body

as the mob drags.

he is standing there as they tells him to come to the castle

as they tell him

The dead has been walking and parading all this time

The dead have been walking

In addition to the errors above, this is all over the place in terms of tenses. The writing goes back and forth between present and past tense, sometimes within the same paragraph, for no apparent reason. Maybe it has something to do with the fluidity of time within whatever this place is, but the end result doesn't really capture that quality; it's merely confusing.

More so than those issues, though, there's a very flashlight-under-the-chin quality to this. This isn't really a narrative, as such. This is supposedly centered on a palace guard and his perspective, but there's no character there, no perspective outside of whatever the reader chooses to bring to the tale. The only thing I know about what is supposedly the central character here is that he sees a lot of scary stuff.

This isn't a story, as much as it's just a collection of images that I've seen before in superior pieces. Those pieces, incidentally, were superior because they were more than just a presentation of spooky imagery. The imagery in those cases served a purpose there other than just existing. If there is a larger purpose, as I take the lengthy "spoiler" section above to suggest, it has little to do with what's actually written here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A tale of a gurad in the City of Alagadda. Huge thanks to those who took a look at it :3

Art by me, but special thanks to 守辰 for helping me with the gif.

Ties in a little with some of my other Alagadda/Hanged King tales, especially And so the Crows Laughed and Of Dragons and Serpents. But I think it won't be too much of a problem if you didn't read them :3 If things goes as plan, I will be posting another tale as a follow-up to this, and a sequel to And so the Crows Laughed soon.

+ Spoilers
- Spoilers

The tales aims to illustrate what happens in Alagadda from an individual's point of view, or what its normal citizens had been through. In this case its a gate guard, who, after the city became what it is now, become disassociated with his actual past. And here in Alagadda, he is and will always be the guard.

The clock isn't actually representing the passage of time, but a countdown. As the number goes from twelve to eventually zero, the city spirals down into, or reveals more and more of its madness. The change of tenses bewteen different sections however, does represent the chaotic flow of time in the city.

The non-italicized text are actual events in Alagadda, or in some cases, the events yet to come. The italizcized text are the guard trying to recall what had happened before the city become what it is. This part is mainly a short retelling of And so the Crows Laughed from the guard's view, except for the Dragon's part. The Dragon, and it's Absence/The God Shaped Hole, is explained in Of Dragons and Serpents's fifth's poem to be Yadabaoth/The Sarkic God, with whom the Hanged King pacted with.

"A parade will be coming as people step on the body of their former Lord. " refers to the event in my tale Midnight Parade, where the Black Lord is casted out of the Court.

A lost traveler could be refering to the Wandsman in A Wandsman in the Court of the Hanged King, but could also be others, like the writer of the dragon peom.

The end of the tale predicts the end of the Alagadda, where the Dragon, who has received enough offering from the city, broke free of its cage and destoryed the city and its inhabitants. The Guard is an unfortunately soul who happened to stumble upon the dragon/its absence, and is bound by the duty to guard the place.
